title: Ultrasounds Save Lives Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill makes new rules for doctors who perform abortions. Before doing an abortion, doctors must do an ultrasound test and show the results to the woman. They also have to give her information about health risks and how the fetus is growing, then wait at least 24 hours before doing the procedure. If doctors don't follow these rules, they can be fined up to $250,000 and women can sue them for money.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Ultrasounds Save Lives Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Requirement of Informed Consent
sections.text: Any doctor or clinic that performs abortions and operates across state lines must follow these rules. Before performing an abortion, except when there is a medical emergency, the abortion provider must do two things. First, they must perform an ultrasound on the woman using whatever method the doctor and patient think is best. The doctor must share the results of this ultrasound with the woman. Second, the doctor must get a signed form called an Informed Consent Authorization form. This form must be given to the woman in person at least twenty four hours before the abortion. The form must include several pieces of information. The doctor must state how many days pregnant the woman is. The doctor must list all the medical risks that come with abortion pills or the specific abortion procedure being used. The doctor must describe the major ways the unborn child has developed at that stage of pregnancy, including whether there is a heartbeat, whether the child can feel pain, and what organs, arms, legs, and facial features have formed. The form must say that an ultrasound was done and the results were shared. The form must explain that these requirements are mandatory for the abortion provider and all medical staff, that breaking these rules can lead to criminal charges and lawsuits, and that a woman can sue if these requirements are not followed. The form must include a statement that everyone signing it has filled it out honestly and understands what it says. The woman seeking the abortion, the abortion provider, and a witness must all sign this form in person. The abortion provider must keep the signed form in the patient's medical records. The same rules that apply to keeping other medical records must also apply to keeping these consent forms. These requirements do not apply if following them would create a greater risk of the pregnant woman dying or suffering major permanent physical harm to an important body function. Mental or emotional conditions do not count as exceptions. If an abortion provider knowingly breaks these rules, the Attorney General can sue them in federal court. For a first violation, the court can fine the provider between one hundred thousand dollars and one hundred fifty thousand dollars for each violation. For later violations, the court can fine the provider between one hundred fifty thousand and one dollar and two hundred fifty thousand dollars for each violation. When a fine is ordered, the Attorney General must tell the state medical board that licenses the provider. Pregnant women cannot be punished under this law. A woman or the parent of a minor who had an abortion that violated these rules can sue the abortion provider. They can ask for money to cover all physical and mental injuries caused by the violation. They can also ask for three times the cost of the abortion as additional damages. They can ask for punitive damages as well. However, no money can be awarded if the pregnancy resulted from the woman's own criminal behavior. If the woman wins the lawsuit, the court must order the abortion provider to pay her legal fees. If the abortion provider wins and the court decides the lawsuit was frivolous, the court must order the woman to pay the provider's legal fees. In any lawsuit under this section, no money or legal fees except those mentioned above can be ordered against the woman who had the abortion. This federal law does not prevent states from having their own disclosure requirements for abortions or penalties that are stricter than what this law requires. Nothing in this law stops an abortion provider from giving the required information to a pregnant woman at the same time they get consent under state law, as long as the information is provided at least twenty four hours before the abortion. For this law, abortion means using any tool, medicine, drug, or device to intentionally kill an unborn child of a woman known to be pregnant, or to intentionally end a pregnancy with any purpose other than producing a live birth after the child could survive outside the womb or removing a dead unborn child. Abortion provider means a person licensed to practice medicine and surgery or osteopathic medicine and surgery, or someone else legally allowed to perform abortions. Attempt means actions that represent a major step toward performing an abortion based on what the person believes the situation to be. Minor means someone under eighteen years old. Perform includes causing an abortion through medical or chemical means, including writing a prescription for a drug or device meant to cause an abortion. Reasonable medical judgment means a medical decision that would be made by a careful abortion provider who knows about the case and the possible treatments for the medical conditions involved. Unborn child means a human organism from fertilization until being born alive as defined in federal law. Woman means a female human being whether or not she is eighteen years old yet.
